transcript.text: Doing good.
How's your week been?
Has it been a busy one or not too bad?
meeting is being recorded.
Sorry.
Yeah, it's been busy to say the least.
I feel like it's been meeting after meeting after meeting, just one of those busy weeks where you can't really catch much air between meetings, but it's been good.
Busy is a good thing, I feel like, at the end of the day.
I totally relate and give you a lot of kudos for managing your full-time job while going through our interview process.
So thank you so much for doing that.
Just as a quick intro on my side, I'm the founding AE here at GrowthX, and we just brought on our second salesperson and are hiring our third.
So I'm super excited about that because it was pretty lonely over here being the only person on the sales side.
Yeah, no, I feel that.
I was the first go-to-market hire at a previous...
And so I totally understand where you're coming from on that.
It does get pretty lonely, but it's fun at the same time.
Absolutely.
Absolutely.
And I've been in places where like I had to sell from a script, right?
So it's much better when you've got some freedom to own the process a little bit and, you know, be creative about the way you go about things.
But I'm super excited to connect with you today.
Tucker, you know, told me some good things about the interview that you guys had.

And I think for the call today, you know, really just like getting to know your experience a little better, digging into, you know, some deals that you'd worked on in the past, maybe some experiences that you have with Outbound.

And then definitely want to save us some time where you could ask me any questions that are on your mind about GrowthX, because it is a little hard to understand exactly what we do from our website sometimes.
So happy to be a resource.
But, yeah, I would love to just maybe kick it off by just hearing in your.
Your own words, like why you're interested in the growthx opportunity and a quick walkthrough of like your experience in sales, you know, up to now.
Totally.
Yeah.
So when Tucker reached out to me, wouldn't say I'm like actively looking for a role, but I think when I'm definitely ready to make the move.
So whenever someone reaches out to me, I like to understand the company and the product that I'd be selling.
And if it aligns well, I'm more than happy to have this type of conversation.
But essentially, to backtrack a little bit, I graduated from Chapman in 2019.
I was working my senior year of college, and then I became an account executive when I first graduated at that company called Firebrand Media.
It's like a boutique marketing agency.
We owned a bunch of different publications along like the Southern Orange County coastline.
And so I would essentially sell marketing campaigns into our various publications, depending on who they were trying to target.
Like which audience they were trying to reach.
From there, I first entered my first real tech experience at a company called Moxo.
Moxo was started from the same founders that started WebEx and they were angel investors in Zoom.
So they had a deep history in more of like the collaboration SaaS space and they felt they kind of pioneered the way business was done in the internet era with WebEx.
And so Moxo was created to pioneer the way business is done in more of like the mobile era.
And that's where I worked.
And I started as an SMB account executive.
I was one of the first two people to get promoted to our mid-market team and kind of lead the charge for that segment as well.
And I was there for about four years.
And then I made the jump to, it was actually one of my customers at Moxo.
So she had a startup called Mosaic and she liked me.
So she basically poached me and offered me a job at her startup.
And so I started there as a, the title was manager of business development, but I was really the first.
Go to market hire at that company.
So I first was really just doing all outbound activity, trying to generate as much buzz as possible, hosting webinars, things like that.
And then after about three to six months, we hired two SDRs.
So I started managing them as well as doing full cycle and demoing and all that good stuff as well.
I was there for about a little over a year, I want to say.
And then I came to ContentSquare, which is where I'm currently at.
And ContentSquare is more in like the product analytics space.
So customers come to us to better understand how their users or people are navigating their products or their websites and optimize their funnels and things like that.
And I'm on the mid-market team.
I think I said that already.
But yeah, that's where I am currently.
So sorry for the long-winded answer, but that's kind of like a brief background of who I am and what I've done.
No, it's great.
Yeah, super helpful just to kind of like, you know, I obviously can look in.
Look at LinkedIn, but I love to just kind of hear you walk me through it.
Tell me, what does Moxo do?
When I looked at your profile, I like, what the hell do they do?
They're still trying to figure that out, I think, too.
Because they're backed by Subra and Stanley, who are the founders of WebEx, they're fully self-funded, but they are willing to pour as much money into it as possible.
So when they first started out, it was more of like a client portal solution where businesses could interact with like their external customers, partners, vendors, people like that.
And then, and there was like, like web conferencing, secure messaging, assigning tasks, like task management, things like that.
And then over time, it evolved into creating Moxo flows, which was really to like streamline external processes with those partners and vendors and customers.
But essentially, we provided like a white label app and client.
And portal.
And I think they still do that.
I'm not 100% sure, but it's shifted a lot more to like streamlining workflows and processes with those external people.
Okay, got it.
Yeah, that makes more sense.
And I see like the evolution from like the WebEx, right?
Like what WebEx was doing, sort of like extending that into like a lot of different ways where you could interact with clients, not just like a meeting video conference setting.
Tell me about like what piqued your interest in the GrowthEx opportunity?
You know, like you're not actively looking, but like what excited you about GrowthEx in particular?
Yeah, one, I think it's a good blend of like what I've, my experience and my skill set.
Like I've worked in the marketing, on the marketing side at Firebrand, and then I've worked selling technology, like specifically SaaS solutions.
So I think it's a good kind of marriage of the two and that in all my roles I've targeted.
Chief Marketing Officers and people that I feel like are in your guys' ICP.
So I definitely have a good understanding of what those people care about and what they like to see and things like that.
So I really just think it's a good alignment of my experience and what I'm looking for in my next role.
Okay, nice.
Nice.
I like that.

And on like a sort of similar segue here, like tell me about, you know, with your experience selling to marketers, like you could choose any of the different companies that you've sold at, but help me sort of understand like how you've positioned a product that like one of the companies, products value to a CMO or like a go-to-market leader in one of the deals, you know, that you've like worked in one.
Like how do you position the products to value in particular?
Yeah, I think, I mean, one deal I'm like most proud of, I would say is it's...
One that I outbounded, so I went and found, and it was Citizens Bank.
It was while I was at Moxo.
We did really well in the financial services sector just because of the security and compliance regulations and what Moxo provided.
So when I first reached out to them, was more so like a general reach out.
There wasn't any really use case in mind.
I just know we did well in the banking industry.
And so I was able to get a meeting with them, but it didn't really lead to anything.
And then a couple months later down the road, I saw that they were rolling out their, what was it called?
Like their digital butler program is I think what they named it.
And it was for like their high net worth individuals.

And so I essentially, because I saw like their press release, I reached out with like a much more targeted messaging and verbiage and basically reached out to people I already had spoken to like previously.
And because it was more, sorry, I love that.
Sorry.
no, you're totally good.
I've had like my.
Okay, phone, Bluetooth connected while my like computer is and then like the phone rings and I can't hear who I'm talking to.
I've done that like more times than I can count.
Yeah, no, I appreciate that.

But yeah, so essentially, I just did a really good job of mapping our solution and what like the value that we provided to this specific program that they were rolling out for the high net worth individuals.
The chief marketing officer was involved, but there was a lot of like chief product officers, chief technology officers, those types of people as well.
And then as well as like digital experience people on their side that were all involved in like the conversations to ultimately push it forward.
And it was just one I was most proud about just because of how large of a deal it was, how large of the company it was that I was selling to.
And then just the amount of nuances that were involved throughout the entire sales cycle.
That makes sense.
So like help me understand like how did...
Okay.
Did Moxo help Citizens Bank in particular?
What problems was it solving for them?
Or what was the value of Moxo for Citizens Bank?
Yeah, totally.
So it was more their digital butler program was more for like a white glove, like high touch customer experience.
And that's really like where Moxo fit best was being able to provide that like, white glove, high touch customer experience.
So a lot of banks would essentially take, we had SDKs and essentially embed us into their existing client portals.
And so because they wanted to provide that high touch experience, they took like our chat SDK, our web conferencing SDK, and a few other things and embedded it into their existing systems.
So they could more seamlessly and more, yeah, more seamlessly, essentially interact with those high net worth individuals on their side.
Okay, nice.
So it was about like,
Like, when they had a high net worth individual that was already a customer?
It was already a customer with Citizens Bank?
Totally, yeah.
Already a customer.
Giving them a better experience anytime they were engaging with the bank, had questions, things of that nature.
You're spot on, yeah, exactly.
And then once we rolled out with Moxo Flows, too, they ended up using that for, like, their onboarding to that specific program.
Or if they wanted to open a new trust or a new fund, they would essentially just send out a flow and streamline, like, that onboarding process or whatever that checklist would be.
Okay, nice.
And I'm, like, guessing that, like, their current, was it, like, their mobile app, web app, just wasn't, like, a more modern experience?
Or, like, helped me understand, like, that aspect a little better?
Yeah.
So,

So they already had a mobile app, so they essentially embedded our mobile SDKs into their existing application, and then they also had a web portal that people could access on their computer, so they also took our SDKs for the web portal and embedded them into their existing portal on web as well.

So it was across devices, they could really use it on any device, and yeah, like you were saying, was kind of just to provide that more seamless experience for those people that they care most about because they're spending a lot of money with them.
Makes sense.

I mean, I bank with like PNC and their mobile app is terrible, so I'm not surprised that like the banking industry was a place where you guys had a lot of wins because oftentimes like their apps kind of suck.
I'm a Schools First.
My mom's a teacher, so I was able to join Schools First.
It's like a credit union down here.
It's the same.
I always, like, thought about pitching to them, too, and I actually did pitch to them.
But, yeah, all these banking apps are definitely pretty archaic in the way that they operate.
A hundred percent.
So, like, let's take this example, right?
Like, what were the metrics or the outcomes that you would use to connect what Moxo was doing to what the client, to what Citizen Bank cared about?
You know, like, and I already understand that, you one was a better experience for the high net worth individuals.
But, you know, any, like, metrics where you sort of quantified the value or, like, other outcomes that helped you, you know, land that deal?
Yeah, totally.
So, the main one that they were focused on was, like, their net promoter score.
So, like, how often people actually engaged with them.
They saw, like, a huge increase in because they were making the experience more seamless and easy doing.
They actually had a lot more people engaging with them and opening new funds with them and just doing more things with them.
So that was a huge kind of metric for them on their end.
Moxo was not so much ROI focused just because it was more of the experience and it wasn't so much so like...

Yeah, just like at the beginning, it wasn't so much of like an ROI use case, but once we came out with Flows and they started using us for Flows too, it was about cutting down like their onboarding time.
So how long it took for like when a new customer was ready to get started with them, how long it would take them to get from point A to being a customer or joining the bank.
But yeah, I would say like the net promoter score was probably like the metric that they cared most about, just how many people were engaging.
On a monthly basis, how often, what they were engaging about, like things like that.
Makes sense.
Makes sense.
NPS is going to directly connect to whether they're going to keep those funds with the bank or whether that high net worth individual is going to take all their millions elsewhere.
Exactly.
Yeah.
A hundred percent.
All right.
Let's sort of like switch gears here and go towards the outbounding side since you've had a lot of experience there.
And I love like, you know, the example you use with Citizen Bank, but maybe you can tell me about like an outreach experiment or a campaign that you ran that helped consistently book meetings.
Yeah.
I actually, I was just showing this to a colleague of mine.
If you don't mind, I can share my screen and just show you like an example of one thing that I used to do.
If you don't care.
Love it.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Visuals are great.
Okay, sweet.
Let me share my screen real quick.
So when I, this is another one was.
So when I was at Moxo, I'd present for like RSKO just on my selling process.
But one thing I did was I would send like to both inbounds and even like I use this as like an I can't see anything yet.
It says like, oh, here we go.
Here we go.
I got it now.
I got it now.
Perfect.

All good.
All good.
So, yeah, essentially this is like an inbound example.
So like when someone would first submit an inquiry, I like to basically deliver this message to them.
Just one, to get them to book a meeting with us right away.
And two, just to give them a better sense of like what their app and portal could look like.
But I also use the same methodology for outbound reasonings as well.
So I would essentially, we had a demo environment like on our phone and computer.
And so I would brand my entire demo environment to their specific company.
And I would make like quick links that were associated.
And basically just tailored the entire experience to like what it would look like for them so they could get a better understanding of like what it was that they were kind of inquiring about.
As well as I would create like loom recordings as well for some of these.
So I would basically just have these screenshots up and just make like one minute loom recordings, just talking through like ways that we could add value for them through our solution.
So this was one way that I generated like a lot of kind of buzz and just generated a lot of meetings because it was really just tailored and personalized.
And I think just going through, you know, branding it, they could see like this wasn't just a random outreach.
And I was really like spending my time kind of curating the messaging and the verbiage and only reaching out to people that I thought we could actually, you know, add value for.
This is super cool.
Um, so this is like what they would be logging into if they were a Moxo customer?
Exactly.
Yeah.
So this is like what their clients, this is like showing the client user's perspective.
So like it could be a client or partner, basically just, we call them external users.
So this is showing like what an external user for Shorelight would be seeing if they were coming onto the app or the portal on their phone or computer.
Oh, that's super cool.
So it's like, um, they could have like their own, the company, the client, right?
For Moxo, they might have their own web app, their own mobile app.
Help me understand like how Moxo fits in there.
It's like a client portal in addition to the mobile app.
Can they access it from the mobile app?
Like just like sort of connect the dots a little bit more for me.
Yeah.
So this was really our out of the box solution.
So if, if companies like we had small business companies and mid market companies that use us that.
for you you You you
Didn't have client portals and didn't have a mobile app.
So we had an out-of-the-box solution that was essentially an app, like their own app in the Apple and Google Play stores.
And then they would also get like a web portal that they could put like client login on their website for people to log in that way.
And then like a lot of our enterprise customers and even some of our mid-market customers would already have existing apps and portals.
And I actually have that pulled up too.
So they would take like our SDKs and essentially embed us into like their existing app or web portal.
So like, for example, these people, they already, the DS Credit Union, they already have this dashboard and like their own client portal, but they added this like click to connect button.
And so once the user clicked the click to connect, it would essentially just like load Moxo within their existing portal.
And then the same could be done for like the mobile app.
And then...
then...
There's like also ways where you could just take like pieces of it.

So if you just wanted like our web conferencing SDK, someone put, I think it was Citibank, they put like a click to connect and it was just like, it would load like our web conferencing SDK so they could connect with their like relationship manager essentially.
Yeah, it makes a lot of sense.
What were some of like their alternatives?
Was there like a lot of competitive solutions in this space?
Or was it more of like you had to create urgency because they weren't considering something like this?
And it was like a new addition to their client experience?

Yeah, it was a lot of like, we called it build versus buy scenarios where they were either looking to build it, build like a client portal on their own with their, with their engineering and like product teams, or they would buy like a point solution like ours, as well as like
The SDK play was more for the enterprise.

So a lot of the times they would often just, because it was a lot cheaper, to leverage our SDKs that we've already built instead of building like their own web conferencing kind of solution for themselves.
So there was a lot of like the build versus buy scenario.
And then there were other solutions that were competitive in the space.
They all kind of, there was like financial services portals.
There was a lot more like focus portals, whereas we were more broad and trying to inherit like a number of different industries and ICPs and all that good stuff.
But there was more like industry specific portals that were competitive and out there that we would come across depending on who we were talking to.
Would y'all typically win against the industry specific or did they have like more tailored solutions?
I guess like what made, what made prospects go with Moxo over other options, would you say?
I think case by case, but like, yeah, it really depended.
Yeah.
Like you're saying, it was a lot of case by case, but I would say overall, we would win.
I mean, we obviously lost to like the more tailored point solutions all the time, but we would also win a lot.
It really just depended on the use case.

I think where we won a lot of the times was more on like the UI and like the user friendliness and how easy it would be for their internal teams, as well as their customers to adopt something like this, like things of that nature.
Okay.
Would y'all implement the SDK for them or was that on the customer to do?

So yeah, we would work like hand in hand, like our engineering and customer, like our customer success team essentially would like hold their hand in terms of walking their engineering team through embedding us.
There was a lot of like customization involved.
All that they could do on their end, like with the colors to make it kind of blend in with their existing portal or app.
So we would basically just be there like side by side to get them to implement it.
And then from there, we would be have ongoing like quarterly business reviews and just checking in with them to make sure everything is going the way that we expected it to.
We also had custom development partners that we would leverage, though, if there was any like custom dev work that was needed.
And it was like past the scope of just embedding it into their portal that we would leverage to or basically point their direction that where they could also help.
And they were very familiar with like our APIs and all that, all that good stuff.
Yeah, it's super interesting.
And I'm sure like, you know, leaving like Moxo is trying to figure out like what they want to be, know, find product market fit.
And then you have this cool opportunity to come like level up as like a manager.
at a startup that, you know, you knew the client because you brought, you knew the person because you brought them on as a client directly.
I guess, you know, did you, did, did you leave Moxo because the opportunity is, was that the primary reason?
And what, what ultimately made you decide to leave Mosaic to go to Concept Square?
Yeah, that's a good question.
I honestly was like super on the fence when I first was offered it.
And so I decided not to make the jump to Mosaic.

And then about a year later, I, I kind of restarted that conversation with her because I just felt like when I first joined Moxo, we had all these like visions and plans of like where we wanted to be, you know, two, three years from now.

And I just felt like because our founders already started WebEx and did a bunch of things, Moxo was kind of, they weren't as urgent and like gung ho about, you know, getting this up and off the ground as they were with previous companies.
question is.
Because they've already done it so many times before, I think.
So the urgency and just the speed at which we were winning deals was just not as what I was foreseeing when I first started there, like three years down the line.
And I kind of just felt like I hit my sort of ceiling in terms of like growth at Moxo.
Nothing bad to say about Moxo, though.
Like that was my first experience in SaaS sales.
And I learned so much there.
And I owe a lot of people like a lot of thanks.
But yeah, that's like really what ultimately made me make the jump.
And I thought it was just, it would be cool to work for.
I really like this customer of mine.
And so she basically just was really good at selling me in terms of like the opportunity.
And yeah, just like what we could kind of do together.
And I think what ultimately made me leave Mosaic was I miss selling to like different industries.
Like we were only specifically targeting residential real estate agents.
And so it just.
just.
Got a little boring after a while.
And we weren't making too much progress besides like the metrics that I was sort of focused on.
We weren't closing a lot after I was like booking meetings.
And I think they were just really early on, which doesn't scare me.
But yeah, it was really just the residential real estate focus that I was kind of bored of by the end of it.
And that's what made me join Content Square.
Makes total sense.
I can relate to like I was at North Spire and they're like a platform for managing, you know, a commercial real estate deal for like a VP of development.
And the person who was in charge of sales and sales development and sales enablement, there was one person in charge of everything, basically.
He was an early employee.
And that was the guy who wanted us to sell from a script for the first and second call.
So that was brutal.
Got some PTSD from that one.
also like, you know, it's like nice to not have yourself pigeonholed in like one industry.
For the rest of your career, you know?
Totally.
Yeah.
You just feel kind of like claustrophobic in a sense of like, yeah, it's just nice.
And I miss the creative aspect of selling to different industries and different decision makers and all those things.
So, yeah.
Yeah.
You're like, if I'm doing that, I might as well just become like a real estate agent.
Yeah, seriously.
That's like how I was feeling.
I was like, I feel like I know the residential real estate industry, like the back of my hand at this point.
Did you ever consider like jumping in as like a real estate agent?
I kind of considered that at one point.
I, when I was younger, I used to like out of school, I was more so considering it versus once I like started in the corporate world.
But yeah, I mean, it would be really fun to be a real estate agent.
I just think it's so hard to like differentiate yourself as a residential real estate agent these days.
And I feel like it's moving more towards like not even needing an agent.
So that's why it made me.
Decide not to go down that path.
But yeah, it did seem like a cool opportunity when I was younger.
Yeah, and I love hearing that because like it shows that there was some like intentionality behind joining Mosaic.
You know, it wasn't like you just randomly jumped into real estate.
Like there was a prior interest and that was sort of the same for me.
Okay, one more kind of like series of questions for you here.
It's another like, tell me is, you know, yeah, this is the real one that was like, you know, tell me a story about one of the biggest deals that you've closed.
And the Citizen Bank example is a great example.
But if you could use a different example, that would be awesome.
And just kind of walk me through, right, like from how the deal was sourced to what you did to, you know, build champions, multi-thread, build a business case and ultimately close the deal.
Would love to just, you know, hear that story.
Yeah, so my other big one while I was at Moxo.
It was Kaplan Education, if you've heard of them before.
Yeah, so they're a really massive company.
They essentially were having difficulty generating revenue because before, like prior Kaplan, they were big in like SAT and tutoring and like SAT prep and all that good stuff.
And because the SAT isn't required to go to college anymore, not as many people were using them for like their SAT prep and tutoring services.
And so they had to kind of pivot in the way their business model was designed.
And so they started offering, I think it was called like something as a service, but essentially they would offer companies.

like Growthx.ai could essentially be a customer of Kaplan and then Growthx.ai could offer all of these like growth opportunities to their employees, like whether that be taking classes to further your career or things to just learn the market.
So it became more of like a B2B side use case for Kaplan, more so than like the B2C.
And essentially, they came to us at Moxo to kind of streamline and facilitate the way that they were interacting with those companies.
It was like an over a year-long sales cycle, I would say.
So yeah, like so many decision makers and different types of like ICPs involved throughout the sales cycle.
But I think what ultimately helped me get the deal across the finish line was how close of a relationship I had with my champion.
And that's really what this deal taught me is like a good champion could help you tremendously in so many different ways.
Because he would essentially prep me on like the people.
We probably had 30 different meetings with all different people from Kaplan throughout the deal cycle.
so we would meet like on a weekly cadence.
He would prep me on exactly like what I needed to be saying to those.
People, what those people in the room cared about.

And so I would always just be really dialed in for when I would go into those meetings because me and the champion had such a close relationship and she had such a clear idea of like what they were looking for.
Yeah, I don't know.
I don't want to ramble if you have any like specific questions on that.
No, for sure.
No, appreciate the thoughtfulness.
What was the title of the champion?
Like, what did they do?
So it was director of digital experience was the champion.
His name was Raul.
And yeah, like from the get go, just right out of the gate, he knew he came to us for like a very specific use case.
And so it was all just about tailoring our solution to, you know, just make it fit perfectly for exactly like what they were looking for throughout the tire sales cycle, like technology validation.
And it was, again, like an embedded approach.
So it was also just making sure that whole side of things was feasible and able to look like how they want it to look.
It makes sense.
And what was like your biggest personal accomplishment with that deal in particular?
Like, what are you most proud of?
I mean, I guess besides closing it, I would just say it's like all the little lessons I learned throughout the deal cycle.
It was definitely like the biggest company that I worked with as selling SaaS technology.
So there was just a lot of nuances and things I learned throughout the deal cycle.
So I would say that's also one thing like I'm proud of is just how much I grew from that entire experience and working with a company of that size.
Yeah, it definitely takes some determination and persistence to keep powering through, you know, 30 meetings over a year of trying to get to the finish line.
Anything that
You, like, wish you would have done differently in hindsight?
That's a good question.
I would say towards the beginning, I expected, because we were talking to so many different people, I was kind of expecting that their side was more aligned.
And I kind of just assumed that their side was aligned on, like, exactly what they were looking for.
But because it's such a big company, they're not talking as much as I would have assumed that they are.

And so I think just making sure, like, their side is aligned as much as possible and not just assuming that they are is something that I could have done differently and something I've taken with me to, like, the next deals that I've worked of that size, like a similar size.
Yeah, for sure.
We have to be so thorough, right?
We have to, like, ask the questions that we even assume are, like, obvious answers, especially when it comes to, like, discovery, right?
Because procurement can be a nightmare.
The time that a legal review.
The
Or maybe a step in the process that you didn't know about could be the difference between hitting your quota that quarter, right?
Or having to push a deal to the next quarter.
So I definitely get it.
How do you feel like your manager would have rated you 1 to 10 on the Kaplan deal?
I would say probably a 10 out of 10 just because of like he knows how much effort and time I put into that deal.
And I think I was really doing everything I possibly could control on like my side to make sure the deal went as smoothly and as seamlessly as possible.
And there was like a lot of like weird hour meetings, like super early in the morning meetings and super late night meetings because they were all over the world, the people that we were talking to.
So I would say he would have given me at least a 10 for effort.
And I think I really leaned into just like product knowledge at Moxo.
So I think that's what contributed to like my success there.
So I was really good at like...
We tailoring our solution to their specific use case, whoever, whether that's Kaplan or whatever company I was kind of speaking to.
